core_intuitions: The chunk-size quality-recall tradeoff: chunks too large bury the relevant sentence in noise (high recall, low precision); chunks too small strip away the referent so a sentence like 'It increased by 12%' becomes meaningless (high precision, lost context). The sweet spot is a crossover, and it is domain-dependent.

Late chunking inverts the order: encode the WHOLE document once so self-attention gives every token full-document context, THEN pool per chunk span. So a pronoun's embedding already knows its referent.

Hybrid search works because BM25 (lexical) and dense (semantic) are complementary failure-wise: BM25 nails rare exact tokens (a CVE id, a SKU) that a generalizing embedding buries; dense nails paraphrases that share no surface tokens.

RRF fuses ranked lists using ONLY rank ordinals via 1/(k+rank), never the raw scores. That is precisely why it is immune to the scale mismatch between BM25 scores (~0-20) and cosine similarities (~0.5-1.0); k=60 keeps any single top-1 from dominating.

The two-stage retrieve-then-rerank funnel: cheap bi-encoder recall over millions, then an expensive cross-encoder that lets every query token attend to every document token, applied to only a small shortlist.

HyDE converts hard query->document matching into easier document->document matching: a short colloquial query sits far from formal answer docs in embedding space, but an LLM-written hypothetical answer lands inside that doc cluster; you embed the hypothesis (and discard its text).

existing.name: chunkrerank-late-chunking-arch
existing.issue: Renders correctly and legibly in both light and dark. Reading order is top-to-bottom and unambiguous; the 'encode the FULL document once' message and the span->mean-pool->chunk_emb columns clearly convey that pooling boundaries are applied AFTER contextual encoding. Self-attention curved dashes at the top reinforce 'every token sees all others'. No clipping, no illegible overlap. Fully understandable as a static frame.
existing.improvement: Optional only: the two-word visual contrast against naive 'chunk-first-then-embed' lives only in the caption; a tiny inline 'vs naive: boundaries applied before encoding -> no cross-context' ghost note would sharpen the payoff, but this is not required.

existing.name: chunkrerank-two-stage-rerank-pipeline
existing.issue: The funnel (100 candidates -> top-5, with the dot-grid count badges) and the fast/slow contrast read very well in both themes, and 'every query token attends to every document token' is the right load-bearing annotation. The only problem is the hardcoded latency figures '~10 ms' and '~200 ms' on the stage tags: those are invented benchmark numbers, which the drawing constraints prohibit, and they also disagree with the chapter's own code-comment numbers (60 ms / 400 ms).
existing.improvement: Replace the absolute millisecond figures with purely relative tags: keep 'fast' / 'slower' (or 'cheap recall' / 'expensive precision', or a '~N candidates' vs '~1 pass each' cost cue) and drop the specific ms values. Everything else stays as-is.

new_figures.name: chunkrerank-chunk-size-tradeoff
new_figures.concept: How chunk size trades precision against recall/context, with a crossover sweet spot.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter opens by calling chunking 'the single highest-leverage RAG decision' and the 'most common cause of poor RAG performance', yet no figure makes the core tension tangible. The tradeoff is inherently spatial (same fact, three granularities; two curves crossing) and a single static frame nails it far better than prose. This is the chapter's foundational lever and is currently unserved.
new_figures.placement: Section 1 'Why Chunking Is the Hardest Part Nobody Talks About' (place the marker at the end of section 1, just before '## 2. Chunking Strategies').
new_figures.brief: A two-part static infographic built around ONE running example sentence so the reader sees the same fact under three chunk sizes. Example context: a short passage whose key sentence is 'It increased by 12%' preceded by its referent 'Q4 revenue hit a record.' TOP ROW: three side-by-side chunk cards over a horizontal 'chunk size' axis (small on the left -> large on the right). (a) LEFT card 'Too small': a single isolated chunk containing only 'It increased by 12%.', with 'It' and '12%' circled/underlined and a small callout 'referent lost -- 12% of what?'. Sub-labels: precision HIGH, context LOST. (b) MIDDLE card 'Sweet spot': a chunk that holds BOTH the referent sentence and the key sentence together; a small check/balanced marker; sub-label 'both signals present (domain-dependent)'. (c) RIGHT card 'Too large': a big chunk where the key sentence is one accent-highlighted line among ~6 muted gray filler lines representing unrelated sentences; callout 'signal buried in noise -> LLM may ignore or hallucinate'. Sub-labels: recall HIGH, precision LOW. BOTTOM: a compact line chart sharing the same left->right 'chunk size' axis: two curves crossing -- a 'precision' curve descending as size grows (use accent) and a 'recall / context sufficiency' curve rising as size grows (use accent-2), crossing in the middle with a soft vertical 'sweet spot' band aligned under the middle card. Label the crossing region 'optimal band is domain-dependent'. Keep numbers to at most small illustrative tick labels; invent no benchmarks. Theme-safe: chunk cards use neutral v-fill/v-stroke, the highlighted key sentence uses an accent tint, filler lines use muted gray; both curves must be distinguishable in light and dark via the accent/accent-2 vars plus differing dash. Everything inside the viewBox, clear top-to-bottom then curves reading order.

new_figures.name: chunkrerank-rrf-fusion
new_figures.concept: Why BM25 and dense are complementary, and how RRF fuses them by rank ordinal so score-scale mismatch can't corrupt the result.
new_figures.rationale: Section 3 is the conceptual center of the chapter and is served only by a numeric table. The two load-bearing intuitions -- lexical/semantic complementarity, and 'RRF uses ranks not scores, hence immune to the 0-20 vs 0.5-1.0 scale mismatch' -- are spatial (two lanes merging by position) and do not come through in a table. A single static frame makes both click.
new_figures.placement: Section 3.1 'Reciprocal Rank Fusion' (place the marker right after the RRF formula and the k=60 sentence, before the code block).
new_figures.brief: A left-to-right fusion diagram with three columns. LEFT-TOP lane 'BM25 (lexical)': a vertical ranked list of five docs (D2, D1, D3, D4, D5 top-to-bottom to match the chapter's worked example) each with an illustrative raw score in the 0-20 band shown to the right (e.g. 18.x, 9.x, 6.x, 3.x, 1.x); annotate the rank-1 doc with a small tag 'exact-token win: e.g. a CVE id / SKU'. LEFT-BOTTOM lane 'Dense (semantic)': the same five docs in a different order (D1, D3, D2, D5, D4) each with an illustrative cosine score in the 0.5-1.0 band (0.9x, 0.8x, ...); annotate its rank-1 doc with 'paraphrase win: no shared surface tokens'. Use accent for the BM25 lane and accent-2 for the dense lane. MIDDLE column: a 'discard scores, keep only rank' gate -- draw the raw scores being struck through / faded and replaced by rank chips 1,2,3,4,5; next to two example ranks show the reciprocal weight with k=60: rank1 -> 1/(60+1), rank2 -> 1/(60+2). Emphasize visually that the two different score SCALES both collapse to the same 1..5 rank ladder. RIGHT column 'Fused (RRF)': the re-sorted list where each doc's bar = sum of its two reciprocal weights; show a doc that was BM25-rank-2 and dense-rank-1 (D1) rising to fused rank 1, with a thin connector from both source lanes. PROMINENT callout banner across the bottom or side: 'RRF never compares 0-20 BM25 scores to 0.5-1.0 cosine scores -- it only sums rank positions, so the scale mismatch can't corrupt fusion; k=60 keeps any single top-1 from dominating.' Use only the small illustrative integers/ranks already consistent with the chapter's worked example; do not invent benchmark magnitudes beyond order-of-magnitude score bands. Theme-safe var-driven colors, no clipping, clear three-column reading order.

new_figures.name: chunkrerank-hyde-embedding-space
new_figures.concept: HyDE turns query->document matching into document->document matching by generating a hypothetical answer that lands near the real answer docs.
new_figures.rationale: Section 5.2 states the intuition purely in words ('a hypothetical answer document and a real answer document inhabit closer regions of embedding space'). That is a geometric claim best shown, not told: a query as an outlier vs a hypothesis teleported into the answer cluster. It is a distinct, high-value intuition not covered by any existing figure.
new_figures.placement: Section 5.2 'HyDE -- Hypothetical Document Embeddings' (place the marker just after the 'The intuition:' paragraph, before the code block).
new_figures.brief: A single 2D 'embedding space' panel drawn as a soft rounded rectangle (boundary via v-stroke). Inside, cluster several (4-5) 'real answer document' dots close together in one region, labeled 'real answer docs (formal / technical vocabulary)'; give them a faint shared halo to read as a neighborhood. Place the raw QUERY as a single distinct marker (accent-2) far from that cluster, near an opposite corner, labeled 'raw query (short, colloquial)'; draw a long dashed line from query to the nearest real doc labeled 'query<->doc gap: far'. Then show the HyDE step: a small arrow from the query marker to an LLM glyph, and from the LLM to a HYPOTHESIS marker (accent, a distinct shape such as a hollow diamond) that lands INSIDE or on the edge of the real-doc cluster; draw a short solid line from the hypothesis to the nearest real doc labeled 'hyp<->doc: close'. Add a caption-style note inside or beside the panel: 'embed the hypothesis (not the query); retrieve the real docs in its neighborhood.' Include a small muted footnote echoing the chapter's warning: 'the hypothesis text is used only for its embedding -- never fed to the generator.' Keep it to a schematic scatter with no numeric axes (label axes only abstractly, or omit). Theme-safe: real docs neutral fill, query accent-2, hypothesis accent, dashed vs solid to distinguish the far gap from the close match; must be legible and non-overlapping in both light and dark; everything inside the viewBox.

summary: Chapter is code-rich and concept-rich across 8 sections but had only 2 figures, both good. Late-chunking architecture: KEEP (correct, legible in both themes, right emphasis on encode-once-then-pool). Two-stage rerank funnel: REFINE (excellent funnel and 'every query token attends every doc token' framing, but drop the invented '~10 ms'/'~200 ms' latency numbers -- prohibited benchmark values that also conflict with the chapter's own 60/400 ms comment; replace with relative fast/slower tags). Proposed 3 new STATIC figures for the foundational intuitions left unserved: (1) chunk-size precision/recall tradeoff with a crossover sweet spot -- the chapter's own 'highest-leverage decision', priority 1; (2) RRF fusion showing BM25/dense complementarity plus the rank-ordinal, scale-immune fusion mechanism -- the heart of section 3, priority 1; (3) HyDE embedding-space geometry showing the query outlier vs the hypothesis teleporting into the answer-doc cluster -- priority 2. All three are single-frame spatial/structural intuitions; no animation is warranted.
